risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were assigned to the programme or the no-treatment control group directly after the baseline measurement using stratified randomisation. This randomisation was performed in blocks of two on the basis of one prognostic factor: the level of concern about falls (some, regular, often, and very often). Computerised alternative allocation was used in the randomisation process, which was performed by an external agency." Comment: The study describes a structured randomization process with stratification, block randomization, and a computerized allocation process run by an external agency. These context clues support that a proper random allocation sequence was generated.

Quote: "Participants were assigned to the programme or the no-treatment control group directly after the baseline measurement using stratified randomisation. This randomisation was performed in blocks of two on the basis of one prognostic factor: the level of concern about falls (some, regular, often, and very often). Computerised alternative allocation was used in the randomisation process, which was performed by an external agency." Comment: This describes the randomisation process and notes involvement of an external agency, but it does not explicitly state a concealment mechanism such as central telephone/web allocation, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes. Because the report does not clearly explain how the person enrolling participants was prevented from foreseeing the next assignment, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were assigned to the programme or the no-treatment control group directly after the baseline measurement..." and "The AMB-Home programme was facilitated by eight trained nurses..." Comment: Because the comparison was an active in-home/telephone behavioural programme versus a no-treatment control, participants and the personnel delivering the intervention would necessarily know allocation. This lack of blinding is likely to affect the dominant outcomes, which were largely subjective and self-reported (e.g. "concerns about falls," "avoidance of activity," anxiety, depression, quality of life). Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "Participants were assigned to the programme or the no-treatment control group..." and "Data for the effect evaluation is gathered by means of telephone interviews which are conducted by trained interviewers, who are blinded for group allocation." and "The primary outcomes... Concerns about falls are assessed by the 16-item Falls Efficacy Scale-International (FES-I). Participants are asked to indicate..." Comment: Outcome assessment involved both blinded interviewers and patient self-report. The most vulnerable outcomes were subjective self-reported measures (fear of falling, activity avoidance, disability, anxiety/depression, quality of life, etc.). Because the comparison was programme versus no-treatment control, participants would know their allocation, and for self-reported outcomes the patient is effectively the assessor. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective self-report outcomes makes this domain high risk overall, even though interviewers were blinded and some outcomes (e.g. falls calendar) are more objective.

Summary: This article is a study design/protocol paper and does not report observed post-randomization attrition numbers for the analysed outcomes at 5 or 12 months by trial arm. It states planned follow-up and intended handling only: "Non-compliant participants of the programme group are approached for all follow-up measurements, and participants with missing data are contacted to ensure completion of data" and "Data of the effect evaluation will be analysed according to both the intention-to-treat and per-protocol principles." It also mentions an expected rather than observed loss: "a dropout rate of 20% during the study is expected." Comment: Because no actual per-arm randomized-versus-analysed numbers, reasons for missing outcome data, or outcome-specific follow-up counts are reported, attrition bias on the outcomes of interest cannot be judged as low or high from this paper.
